title: Trump Ends Situation Room Meeting Without Iran Deal Announcement
subhead: President Trump convened his national security team in the White House Situation Room to make a 'final determination' on a 60-day ceasefire framework with Iran, but the meeting concluded without any announcement — as Israel crossed the Litani River in Lebanon and a Russian drone injured two Romanians in the first NATO territory casualties of the Ukraine war.

sections.heading: Trump's 'Final Determination' Meeting Ends Without Decision
sections.body:
President Trump met with top national security advisers in the White House Situation Room on Friday to make what he called his 'final determination' on a framework agreement with Iran — but several hours later, the White House confirmed only that the meeting had concluded, providing no details on next steps.

According to BBC and Al Jazeera, the tentative memorandum of understanding would extend the current ceasefire by 60 days, reopen the Strait of Hormuz to commercial shipping, and launch new negotiations on Iran's nuclear program. But deep mistrust remains on both sides. Iran's foreign ministry spokesman said there was 'no final agreement,' and chief negotiator Mohammad Baqer Qalibaf declared that Tehran judges agreements by 'actions, not words' — adding that 'the winner of any agreement is the one who is better prepared for war the day after.'

Trump's public statement before the meeting was maximalist: Iran must 'never have a nuclear weapon or bomb,' Hormuz must be 'open in both directions and without tolls,' all mines must be destroyed, and the US must be allowed to 'remove and destroy' Iran's enriched uranium. Iranian state media pushed back, with Fars News Agency calling Trump's comments a 'mixture of truth and lies' and stressing there was no provision to destroy nuclear materials in the MOU.
sections.whyItMatters: The longer Trump delays, the more time both sides have to find reasons to walk away. The framework represents the closest the US and Iran have come to ending the three-month war that has disrupted global oil flows and redrawn Middle East alliances. But Trump's public demands — particularly on uranium destruction — go beyond what Iran has agreed to, suggesting either a negotiation gap or domestic political theater. If the deal collapses, the ceasefire could fracture, returning the region to open conflict.

sections.heading: Russian Drone Injures Civilians in Romania, First NATO Casualties
sections.body:
A Russian Geran 2 drone (the Iranian-designed Shahed 136) struck a residential building in the Romanian town of Galați, injuring two civilians — the first time Romanians have been hurt in the Ukraine war. About 70 people were evacuated as fire crews extinguished the blaze on the 10th floor of the apartment block.

Romanian President Nicușor Dan said the drone was likely hit by Ukrainian air defenses over Kyiv's territory, altering its trajectory before crashing in Romania. He announced Romania would expel the Russian consul in Constanța and shut down the consulate, calling it 'the most serious incident to have affected Romanian territory since the start of Russia's war of aggression against Ukraine.'

NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte said the alliance 'stands ready to defend every inch of allied territory.' EU Commission President Ursula von der Leyen said Russia's war had 'crossed yet another line.' Putin deflected responsibility, suggesting the drone wreckage should be handed to Russia for 'objective investigation.'
sections.whyItMatters: This is the first time the Ukraine war has physically harmed NATO civilians on alliance territory. While Romania is treating it as an accidental spillover rather than a deliberate attack, it forces NATO to consider whether its air defenses and response protocols are adequate. The incident comes at a sensitive moment: European allies are already questioning US security commitments, and any escalation in how NATO handles drone incursions could draw the alliance deeper into the conflict.

sections.heading: Israel Added to UN Sexual Violence Blacklist
sections.body:
The United Nations placed Israel on its blacklist of countries suspected of committing conflict-related sexual violence, alongside Russia, which was also newly added. The report documented 'multiple incidents' of sexual violence against 31 Palestinian victims — including rape, gang rape, genital mutilation, and threats of rape — primarily during detention and interrogation at military facilities, checkpoints, and during operations in Gaza and the occupied West Bank.

Israel's UN Ambassador Danny Danon said the country would sever all ties with UN Secretary-General Antonio Guterres over the decision, dismissing the allegations as 'ridiculous.' The UN's Pramila Patten said Israel had not provided any information on preventive measures or accountability.

The report also documented 310 verified cases of sexual violence by Russian forces in Ukraine, including rape, gang rape, genital mutilation, and electric shocks to genitals, affecting 280 men, 26 women, and four girls. Nearly 10,000 cases of conflict-related sexual violence were recorded worldwide in 2025 — more than double the previous year.
sections.whyItMatters: Being added to the UN blacklist carries reputational damage and can bar countries from UN peacekeeping operations. For Israel, already facing international criticism over its conduct in Gaza and Lebanon, this adds to the diplomatic pressure. For Russia, it confirms a pattern of systematic abuse documented throughout the Ukraine war. The doubling of recorded sexual violence cases globally reflects both improved documentation and the brutal nature of current conflicts.

sections.heading: Israel Crosses Litani River as Pentagon Hosts Lebanon Talks
sections.body:
Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu confirmed that Israeli forces have crossed the Litani River in southern Lebanon — pushing deeper into territory beyond the UN-patrolled zone even as Israeli and Lebanese military delegations met at the Pentagon for security talks. Reuters and The Times of Israel report troops are advancing on multiple fronts, with Israeli strikes killing at least 14 people in Lebanon on Friday alone.

The Lebanon death toll has surpassed 3,100 since March 2, with over 9,400 injured. Israel has issued new evacuation orders for areas beyond the Litani, suggesting the offensive may expand further. France urged Israel to 'refrain' from taking control of areas in southern Lebanon, but Netanyahu has vowed to continue operations against Hezbollah regardless of diplomatic developments.

The disconnect between the Pentagon talks — focused on security arrangements — and Israel's expanding ground operations highlights the gap between US diplomatic efforts and Israeli military objectives. Israeli Defense Minister Israel Katz said troops would destroy 'all houses' near the Lebanon border as part of creating a new 'buffer zone.'
sections.whyItMatters: Israel's push past the Litani signals that even if a US-Iran deal reopens Hormuz, the Lebanon front will continue independently. The parallel tracks — Pentagon talks and expanding ground operations — suggest Israel is negotiating security arrangements while simultaneously creating facts on the ground. The death toll and displacement are creating a humanitarian crisis that will outlast any ceasefire in the Iran theater.

sections.heading: US Defense Secretary Signals Readiness to Resume Iran Strikes
sections.body:
Speaking at a security summit in Singapore, US Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said American military stockpiles are 'more than suited' to resume strikes against Iran if necessary, adding that the US is 'in a very good place' regarding munitions. The comments came as the USS Nimitz carrier strike group remains positioned near the Gulf.

On Thursday, Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps said it targeted a US air base in Kuwait that was 'the source' of earlier strikes on Bandar Abbas. US Central Command called the attack an 'egregious ceasefire violation.' Both sides have accused each other of violating the ceasefire in recent days.

Meanwhile, Trump said ships 'caught in the Strait due to our amazing and unprecedented Naval Blockade, which will now be lifted, may start the process of 'heading home!'' — suggesting that even without a final deal, some de-escalation of the maritime standoff may begin.
sections.whyItMatters: Hegseth's comments signal that military options remain on the table if diplomacy fails. The continued accusations of ceasefire violations — and actual strikes like the Kuwait attack — show how fragile the truce remains. But Trump's suggestion that ships could begin moving suggests the US is preparing to unwind the naval blockade even before a formal agreement, a potential confidence-building measure that could help move negotiations forward.

bottomLine: Day 91 of the Iran war and the diplomatic moment hangs in the balance. Trump called his national security team to the Situation Room for a 'final determination' on a ceasefire framework — then ended the meeting without announcement. Iran says there's 'no final agreement' and that it trusts only actions, not words. The longer the delay, the more room exists for spoilers on both sides to derail a deal. Meanwhile, Israel pushed past the Litani River in Lebanon even as its officials met at the Pentagon, and Netanyahu made clear the offensive will continue regardless of what happens with Iran. In Romania, a Russian drone injured two civilians — the first NATO territory casualties of the Ukraine war — prompting Romania to expel the Russian consul and raising questions about alliance response protocols. The UN added both Israel and Russia to its sexual violence blacklist, with Israel saying it will cut ties with the Secretary-General. And in Singapore, Defense Secretary Hegseth reminded everyone that the US arsenal remains ready if diplomacy fails. The coming hours will determine whether Trump signs off on the Iran framework or lets the moment pass — but the Middle East's other conflicts are not waiting for Washington's decision.
